coredata.dc:title: Doping of superalkali and superhalogen on graphene quantum dot surfaces to enhance nonlinear optical response: An efficient strategy for fabricating novel electro-optical materials
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Umar, Ali
Yaqoob, Junaid
Khan, Muhammad Usman
Hussain, Riaz
Alhadhrami, A.
Almalki, Abdulraheem S.A.
Janjua, Muhammad Ramzan Saeed Ashraf
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots are evolving as a popular novel class of organic nonlinear optical (NLO) materials, with applications in optoelectronics, nanocomposites, and many other areas. Tetragonal graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) doped with superalkalis (Li3O, Li4N) and superhalogens (BeF3, BF4) have been investigated here with regard to their geometrical, thermodynamic, electronic, and NLO properties by means of density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP level. Computational studies reflect the high stability of these complexes, and indicate interaction with the dopants at the top of a cavity. Interaction energies of dopant molecules suggest the feasibility of their complexation on a T-GQD sheet, with an isomer of BF4@T-GQD showing the highest interaction energy (Eint) of −97.15 kcal mol−1. HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (EH-L) yield the lowest band gap (0.23 eV) for an isomer of Li3O@T-GQD, which in turn enhances the NLO response. A significant increase in NLO response is noted for all of the reported complexes, particularly for superalkali-doped Li4N@T-GQD, which displays a first hyperpolarizability (β o) of 4.5 × 104 a.u. Moreover, absorption phenomena prove that the resultant complexes retain viable deep-ultraviolet (UV) transparency. Natural bond order (NBO) and density of states (DOS) analyses further verify charge transfer and the establishment of new states in the complexes. All of the results manifest the distinction of superalkali- and superhalogen-doping on T-GQD, with superalkali being an electron-donating entity and superhalogen showing electron-withdrawing behavior. We conclude that superalkali-doped Li4N@T-GQD and superhalogen-doped BF4@T-GQD complexes (isomers f and l) may serve as excellent alternatives to current NLO materials. Hence, this study may pave the way for the fabrication of thermally stable graphene quantum dot complexes as valuable precursors for highly efficient NLO materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetragonal graphene quantum dot (T-GQD)
Superhalogen
Superalkali
Nonlinear optics (NLO)
Density functional theory (DFT)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of Taif University Researchers Supporting Project number ( TURSP-2020/47 ), Taif University , Taif, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of T-GQDs doped with Li3O and Li4N (a–f) and BeF3 and BF4 (g–l).
Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces of undoped T-GQD and SA- and SH-doped complexes.
DOS spectra of isolated T-GQD and SA- and SH-doped T-GQD.
Comparison of polarizabilities and hyperpolarizabilities of isolated T-GQD and its SA-doped complexes.
Comparison of polarizabilities and hyperpolarizabilities of isolated T-GQD and its SH-doped complexes.
UV/Vis spectra of bare T-GQD and superalkali-doped complexes.
UV/Vis spectra of bare T-GQD and superhalogen-doped complexes.
Isosurface NCI images in combination with scattering plots between reduced density gradient (RDG) and sign (λ2) for SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.
Interaction energies Eint in kcal mol−1, cohesive energy per atom (EC) in eV, symmetries, and bond distances X for SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.
Vertical ionization energy (VIE) and charge transfer (q) in isolated T-GQD and SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.
FMO analysis, H-L gaps, and charge transfer (Q) in isolated T-GQD and SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.
Quantum chemical descriptors: hardness (ƞ), softness (S), ionization potential (IP), electron affinity (EA), and chemical potentials of bare and doped T-GQD systems.
NLO parameters of polarizability αo, first hyperpolarizability βo, and dipole moment μo for pure and SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.
NLO parameters of first hyperpolarizability β o for pure T-GQD and its SA- and SH-doped complexes calculated by different methods.
Maximum absorption wavelengths (λ max), maximum oscillator strengths (fo), transition energies (ΔE ex), and subjugated transitions of pure T-GQD and its SA- and SH-doped complexes.

head.ce:title: Doping of superalkali and superhalogen on graphene quantum dot surfaces to enhance nonlinear optical response: An efficient strategy for fabricating novel electro-optical materials

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, Dhahran 31261, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots are evolving as a popular novel class of organic nonlinear optical (NLO) materials, with applications in optoelectronics, nanocomposites, and many other areas. Tetragonal graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) doped with superalkalis (Li3O, Li4N) and superhalogens (BeF3, BF4) have been investigated here with regard to their geometrical, thermodynamic, electronic, and NLO properties by means of density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP level. Computational studies reflect the high stability of these complexes, and indicate interaction with the dopants at the top of a cavity. Interaction energies of dopant molecules suggest the feasibility of their complexation on a T-GQD sheet, with an isomer of BF4@T-GQD showing the highest interaction energy (Eint) of −97.15 kcal mol−1. HOMO-LUMO energy gaps (EH-L) yield the lowest band gap (0.23 eV) for an isomer of Li3O@T-GQD, which in turn enhances the NLO response. A significant increase in NLO response is noted for all of the reported complexes, particularly for superalkali-doped Li4N@T-GQD, which displays a first hyperpolarizability (β o) of 4.5 × 104 a.u. Moreover, absorption phenomena prove that the resultant complexes retain viable deep-ultraviolet (UV) transparency. Natural bond order (NBO) and density of states (DOS) analyses further verify charge transfer and the establishment of new states in the complexes. All of the results manifest the distinction of superalkali- and superhalogen-doping on T-GQD, with superalkali being an electron-donating entity and superhalogen showing electron-withdrawing behavior. We conclude that superalkali-doped Li4N@T-GQD and superhalogen-doped BF4@T-GQD complexes (isomers f and l) may serve as excellent alternatives to current NLO materials. Hence, this study may pave the way for the fabrication of thermally stable graphene quantum dot complexes as valuable precursors for highly efficient NLO materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few years, many researchers have turned their attention towards nonlinear optical (NLO) materials owing to the applications of such materials in the fields of photonics, optoelectronics, and many others [1–3]. In view of the significance of nonlinear optics, many approaches for fabricating highly efficient NLO materials have been devised [4]. Organic NLO materials have attracted great attention due to their unique electronic properties and NLO responses, which can be regulated through various structural modifications. Modified graphene has proved to be an exceptional NLO material, being a member of carbon nanosystems alongside fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, and various others, reflecting the potent NLO behavior that arises from extensive delocalization of π-electrons [5].

Graphene possesses a two-dimensional structure with extensive π-conjugation and sp2 hybridization, and as such acts as an effective NLO material after doping with suitable metals [6]. Theoretical and experimental studies have verified the large first-order hyperpolarizabilities besides increased third-order NLO properties. Other than graphene, 12 novel two-dimensional non-hexagonal carbon allotropes have been proposed [7]. Furthermore, Liu et al. proposed tetragonal graphene (T-graphene), which possesses tetragonal symmetry on account of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [8]. T-graphene consists of a two-dimensional sheet fabricating one sub-lattice in its unit cell. Previous work has indicated the direct dependence of the NLO response of organic materials on the extent of delocalization of π-electrons, which can be attained by appropriate substitution to obtain electron-excessive compounds.

One of the effective contemporary strategies for increasing NLO properties is the doping of superalkalis and superhalogens, since this can generate diffuse excess electron systems [9]. Organic materials are doped with superalkalis [10] because of their small cluster size and lower ionization potentials compared to alkali metals. Indeed, this specific character led to such species being named as superalkalis, for example Li4N, Li3O, Li2F, etc. [11].

Moreover, enhanced NLO behavior has been observed by the doping of superhalogens in several recent studies. Superhalogens consist of electronegative atoms (Cl, F, and O) surrounding a central atom. Multiple electronegative atoms each provide electrons, which are delocalized over the central atom, thus increasing the electron affinity (EA) of the molecule. The EA of a superhalogen exceeds that of a halogen atom [12]. Two-dimensional tetragonal silicene (T-Si) and tetragonal germanene (T-Ge) quantum dots (QDs) have been investigated with regard to their NLO responses by Ghosal et al. [13].

Research has revealed that planar T-graphene exhibits improved stability compared to other graphene allotropes, such as graphdiyne and graphyne. However, planar T-graphene quantum dots have yet to be synthesized experimentally. Nevertheless, engineering of graphene-based electronic devices followed by a synthesis route to obtain a single-layer T-graphene has been proposed, which holds promise for the synthesis of T-graphene in the near future [14,15]. Moreover, T-graphene has also been subjected to electronic band analysis, which relates its metallic band structure to its monolayer T-graphene structure, predicting other useful features [16]. We selected the most stable allotrope T-GQD-11, on account of it having the most negative cohesive energy among the 12 proposed allotropes [17], and calculated the effects of doping with superalkalis and superhalogens on its NLO properties [7]. T-graphene quantum dots (T-GQDs) have been widely reported [18–20], and the results of various optical parameters validate their application as optoelectronic devices [21].

In this study, we have investigated the geometrical, thermodynamic, electronic, and NLO properties of superalkali (Li3O, Li4N)- and superhalogen (BeF3, BF4)-doped T-GQDs. The effects of superalkali and superhalogen doping on T-GQDs have been explored, comparing the NLO responses of all of the possible structures after modifying the nature, size, and interaction behavior of the dopant molecules. The current investigation validates the application of superalkali- and superhalogen-doped T-GQDs as efficient NLO materials and opens new horizons for the future design of QD-based NLO materials.

Geometrical optimization of pure T-GQDs, superalkali (SA)-doped T-GQDs (Li4N@T-GQDs and Li3O@T-GQDs), and superhalogen (SH)-doped T-GQDs (BF4@T-GQDs and BeF3@T-GQDs) was performed by employing the B3LYP functional and the 6-31G (d,p) basis set. Various possible geometric arrangements of SAs and SHs on T-GQDs were considered.

Frequency calculations were performed at the same level of theory, which confirmed that all of the optimized structures related to true minima with no imaginary frequencies. Interaction energies (Eint), vertical ionization energies (VIEs), dipole moments (μo), natural bond orbital (NBO) charges, UV/Vis absorption spectra, and HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of the optimized structures were investigated at the same computational level.

Stability was evaluated by calculating the interaction energies of the designed structures according to the following relationship:
(1)
E int = E complex – (E GQD + E dopant)
Where E GQD, E dopant, and E complex are the total energies of undoped GQD, the corresponding SA or SH entity (Li4N, Li3O, BeF3, BF4), and the doped GQD system, respectively.

The following equation was used to calculate the HOMO-LUMO energy gap:
(2)
E H-L = E LUMO–E HOMO
In the above equation, E LUMO is the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and E HOMO is the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO).

VIEs were calculated according to the following relationship:
(3) VIE = E(X+) - E(X)
Where E(X) denotes the energy of the neutral SA@T-GQDs and SH@T-GQDs and E(X+) denotes the energy of the SA+@T-GQDs and SH+@T-GQDs cations.

Gaussum version 3.0 software was utilized to plot the density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) of all the optimized structures [22]. Energy calculations were performed by time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) at the CAM-B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) level.

Moreover, chemical reactivity parameters, such as EA, ionization potential (IP), softness, and hardness [23–27] of the complexes were analyzed on the basis of quantum mechanical parameters, as computed by the following equations. The results are presented in Table 1 .
(4) Electron affinity (EA) = -ELUMO
(5) Ionization potential (IP) = -EHOMO
(6)
S o f t n e s s
( S )
=
1
2 η
(7)
H a r d n e s s
( η )
= I P – E A

Polarizability (a o) and first hyperpolarizability (β o) for all of the optimized structures were evaluated at the CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level of theory, which offers a high level of reliability and is extensively applied in modern nonlinear optics to determine hyperpolarizabilities [24,27–30] according to the following relationships:
(8)
α o = 1/3(αxx+ αyy+ αzz)
(9)
β o = [β x 2 + β y 2 + β z 2]1/2
Where β x = β xxx + β xyy + β xzz,
β y = β yyy+ β yzz+ β yxx
and β z = β zzz+ β zxx+ β zyy

All of the abovementioned calculations were performed with Gaussian 09 [31], and the obtained data were visualized with the GaussView 6.0 program [32]. DFT is evidently the best method for estimating the electronic properties of materials. Furthermore, visual molecular dynamics (VMD) [33] software was deployed in association with Multiwfn [22] software to perform non-covalent interaction index (NCI) analysis [34], which depicts the results as 2D reduced density gradient (RDG) and 3D isosurfaces [35].
The optimized geometry of a bare T-GQD is shown in Fig. 1 .

The T-GQD sheet involves four octagons and five tetragons. Bare T-GQD has C 1 symmetry, that is, centrosymmetry, which is broken upon doping with SAs or SHs. There are three possibilities for dopants to interact with a quantum dot sheet, namely placing the dopant (a) at the center of an octagon, (b) at the center of an internal tetragon, or (c) at the center of an external tetragon. By adopting different combinational schemes, we ultimately obtained three arrangements for each dopant molecule. To model doping, the SAs and SHs were placed horizontally over the T-GQD sheet.
The optimized structures of all of the doped T-GQD isomers are shown in Fig. 2 . Of these possible orientations, we considered the most stable structures for further discussion.

Interaction energy (Eint) is a useful parameter for assessing the stability of a complex, quantifying the interaction between the dopants and the T-GQD. Higher interaction energy signifies greater stability of the complex. Eint values for SA- and SH-doped isomers, along with other stability parameters, are presented in Table 1.

Eint values calculated for Li3O- and Li4N-doped T-GQDs are −60.12, −59.97, −57.67, −58.30, −56.00, and −58.23 kcal mol−1 for a–f, respectively. Eint values calculated for BeF3- and BF4-doped T-GQDs are −56.51, −62.79, −83.50, −77.65, −77.69, and −97.15 kcal mol−1 for isomers g–l, respectively. Thus, the interactions of SAs and SHs with T-GQDs are highly exothermic, giving rise to thermodynamically stable complexes. The most stable isomers are a for Li3O@T-GQD, d for Li4N@T-GQD, i for BF4@T-GQD, and l for BeF3@T-GQD.

Considering the most stable complexes among the respective dopant molecules, their stabilities decrease in the order: BF4@T-GQD (isomer l) > BeF3@T-GQD (isomer i) > Li3O@T-GQD (isomer a) > Li4N@T-GQD (isomer d). Thus, SH-doped T-GQD complexes (BF4@T-GQD, BeF3@T-GQD) are more thermodynamically stable than SA-doped T-GQD complexes (Li3O@T-GQD, Li4N@T-GQD). This may be rationalized in terms of the smaller size of halogen atoms.

NBO analysis was performed to analyze charge transfer in the complexes. The NBO (Q) values indicate that electron transfer occurs between the dopant and the T-GQD sheet in all of the complexes (Li3O@T-GQD, Li4N@T-GQD, BeF3@T-GQD, and BF4@T-GQD). The presence of positive charges on the alkali metal atoms and ultimately the overall positive charge on the SA unit determine the direction of charge transfer. The SA isomers (a–f) exhibit Q values between 0.52 |e| (for e) and 1.06 |e| (for f). This NBO analysis verified charge transfer from the SA dopant molecule to the QD surface in all of the SA-doped complexes.

For SHs, strong electron acceptor behavior is evident from the high EA values (>2.89 eV) for our reported complexes. All of the isomers of electron-withdrawing BeF3 and BF4 demonstrate the same behavior; electrons are drawn from the T-GQD, resulting in a greater negative charge density on these SH molecules. Overall, the SH-doped complexes (g–l) show Q values ranging from −0.95 |e| (for k) to −0.51 |e| (for i), as listed in Table 3
. Charge transfer from T-GQD to the SA dopant establishes the phenomenon of intermolecular electron donor-acceptor (D-π-A) interactions, which in turn contributes to enhancing the β o values of the complexes [36,37].
Hence, NBO results validate the overall charge-transfer phenomena between the dopants (Li3O, Li4N, BeF3, and BF4) and T-GQD sheet [38].

In order to further evaluate the stability of the complexes, VIEs were calculated. A higher VIE value for a complex is indicative of greater chemical stability [39]. VIEs for the reported complexes lie in the range from 5.02 eV to 6.72 eV, as indicated in Table 2. For isolated T-GQD, we calculated a VIE of 5.84 eV. Among the doped complexes, BeF3@T-GQD (isomer g) and BF4@T-GQD (isomer j) exhibited the highest VIEs of 6.72 eV and 6.36 eV, respectively, showing them to be the most stable complexes electronically. On the other hand, complexes Li4N@T-GQD (isomers d and e) showed the lowest VIEs of 5.02 eV and 5.04 eV, respectively. The different VIEs can be attributed to the varying interaction distances between the dopant (SA, SH) and T-GQD, depending on the sizes of the former. Overall, all of the SA- and SH-doped complexes display higher VIEs than the isolated T-GQD, suggesting sufficient electronic stability.

Electronic properties and comparison of electronic parameters of the isolated T-GQD and doped complexes were investigated by FMO analysis, and the results are summarized in Table 3. HOMO and LUMO energies provide valuable insight into post-doping conductivity variation in doped systems since they yield the HOMO-LUMO energy gap (E H-L) [40,41].

The apparent Eg value of T-GQD is 1.64 eV, with HOMO and LUMO values of −4.54 and −2.91eV, respectively. Isomers b, c, d, e, h, j, and k show lower Eg values than the isolated T-GQD, amounting to 0.23, 0.91, 1.63, 1.48, 1.34, 1.34, and 1.34 eV, respectively. All of the other doped complexes show higher Eg values. The incorporation of SA or SH in these complexes has no influence in terms of lowering the band gap. The HOMO and LUMO surfaces for isolated T-GQD and the doped complexes are presented in Figure S1 (Supporting Information). The enormous lowering of Eg in Li3O@T-GQD (isomer b) indicates efficient interaction of the Li3O molecule with T-GQD. This is probably due to the larger size and high number of Li atoms lowering the ionization potential. In the case of Li4N@T-GQD, BeF3@T-GQD, and BF4@T-GQD (a, f, g, i, l), Eg shows a slight increase compared with the isolated T-GQD molecule. This can be ascribed to the higher energy of their HOMOs, as listed in Table 3.

It is preferable to observe the structural modifications in T-GQD after doping with SAs (Li3O, Li4N) and SHs (BeF3, BF4) by employing molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis. A comprehensive 3D representation is obtained, which is helpful for exploring the molecular structure as well as physicochemical aspects of the reported complexes. The negative (electron-rich) region in an MEP representation is shown in red, while the positive region is shown in blue, indicating a deficiency of the electronic cloud. A green coloration delineates a neutral electrostatic potential state [42]. Overall, comparable amounts of the respective colors for a bare T-GQD molecule indicate an equal charge distribution, thus characterizing it as a neutral molecule. All of the SA-doped isomers (a–f) clearly depict a blue spot, verifying the electron-deficient SA molecule, which is rendered positive charge by transferring electron density towards the T-GQD. SH-doped T-GQD complexes (isomers g–i) show a red coloration on the SH molecule as an electron-rich region, confirming the withdrawal of electron density from the T-GQD substrate. Overall, MEP analysis suggests the presence of states suitably predisposed for charge-transfer phenomena giving rise to an increased dipole moment, which is responsible for the enhanced NLO response. MEP representations of bare T-GQD and of SA- and SH-doped T-GQDs are provided in Fig. 3 .

Upon complexation, the energy states of a surface undergo slight alteration. This change in the electronic states after SA- and SH-doping has been comprehensively examined by density of states (DOS) analysis [43]. DOS plots for the most stable isomers of SA- and SH-doped T-GQDs are presented in Fig. 4 .

As indicated in Fig. 4, the HOMO and LUMO energies for isolated T-GQD lie at −4.54 eV and −2.91 eV, respectively. Subsequent doping with SH or SA gives rise to new energy states, as observed in the DOS plots. The generated HOMO states after doping with SA or SH have higher values than for isolated T-GQD, as indicated in Fig. 4, which is a consequence of the aforementioned electron transfers, and implies increased conductivity of the doped systems.
The electronic properties of the complexes were further probed through quantum chemical descriptors. These parameters are listed in Table 4 .

Chemical hardness (ƞ) values indicate that complexation of SAs or SHs with T-GQD slightly decreases the hardness of the complexes, in turn implying enhanced chemical reactivity. With the lowest ƞ value, the Li3O@T-GQD complex (isomer c) is predicted to be the most reactive of the reported complexes. The electron-donating character of isomer c is accordingly larger on account of its lowest IP. IP values for stable isomers of the complexes Li3O@T-GQD, Li4N@T-GQD, BeF3@T-GQD, and BF4@T-GQD were calculated as 4.06 eV, 3.83 eV, 5.03 eV, and 4.81 eV, respectively. In contrast, the EA values for the stable isomers of Li3O@T-GQD, Li4N@T-GQD, BeF3@T-GQD, and BF4@T-GQD were calculated as 2.40 eV, 2.20 eV, 3.13 eV, and 2.89 eV, respectively. Electron-withdrawing character directly depends on EA and EN values. The ability of doped T-GQDs to transfer electrons is noticeably higher than that of isolated T-GQD. Global reactivity indices for all of the isomers are presented in Table 4. All of the pertinent parameters validate the applicability of the reported complexes as effective NLO materials.

NLO properties were probed by analyzing dipole moments (μ), polarizability (α o), and first hyperpolarizability (β o). The effects of excess electrons on the NLO properties of a molecule were recognized in earlier reports as these excess electrons occupy the higher energy HOMO states, narrowing the band gap E g and increasing β o values [44]. An effective approach for enhancing NLO response is doping with SA or SH molecules. The NLO properties of T-GQD and doped complexes were explored by DFT calculations of the aforementioned parameters at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) and CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) levels. Comparatively higher NLO parameters were obtained at the CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level, with the remarkable NLO responses of the relevant complexes indicated in Table 5 .

Pristine T-GQD shows a centrosymmetric structure, as evidenced by the near-zero value of β o . Upon complexation with SAs and SHs, both μo and β o show marked increases due to the distortion of this centrosymmetry. The introduction of SA or SH imparts excess electrons into the system, which in turn breaks the centrosymmetry of the molecule, causing an increase in μo due to charge transfer from SA and SH (donors) to the T-GQD sheet (acceptor). Among the various isomers, the highest β o values were observed for Li3O@T-GQD (isomer c), Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f), BeF3@T-GQD (isomer h), and BF4@T-GQD (isomer l), amounting to 2.1 × 104, 4.5 × 104, 2.5 × 104, and 2.6 × 104 a.u., respectively.

For the isomers exhibiting the highest β o values, they increase in the order Li3O@T-GQD (isomer c) < BeF3@T-GQD (isomer h) < BF4@T-GQD (isomer l) < Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f). Among all of the studied complexes, Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f) exhibits the largest α o and β o values of 515 and 4.5 × 104 a.u., respectively, as is evident from Fig. 5 (a and b).
The reason for the exceptionally high β o value for this complex is the presence of a large number of Li atoms in the dopant molecule.
The β o values of all of the T-GQD complexes calculated by different methods are compared in Table 6 . The results suggest that CAM-B3LYP is the most precise method.

Overall, CAM-B3LYP gave higher β o values, and we used it in further work. The effect on β o values of the doping with SA or SH was analyzed by the well-known two-level model [45,46], as expressed below:
(10)
β o ∝ Δμ fo/ΔE 3

Where Δμ represents the difference in dipole moment between the ground state and the crucial excited state, fo is the oscillator strength, and ΔE denotes the crucial excitation energy. As is apparent from the above two-level expression, β o increases as Δμ and fo are increased, whereas it shows an inverse trend with the third power of ΔE. Thus, ΔE is the decisive factor in determining βₒ values [47]. Therefore, we also calculated crucial transition energies of undoped T-GQD as well as its complexes, and the results are summarized in Table 5. The transition energy for undoped T-GQD was evaluated as 2.6094 eV, decreasing to 2.216 eV for the Li4N@T-GQD isomer. Similarly, transition energies of 2.211 and 2.205 eV were calculated for the BF4@T-GQD isomers. Thus, the two-level model supports the increment in NLO response values of the doped complexes due to lower transition energies as compared to pristine T-GQD.

All of the NLO parameters suggest that SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes may serve as efficient and emerging alternatives to current NLO materials, with Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f) exhibiting the most promise.

Frequency-dependent β o values were calculated to evaluate the practicality of the studied complexes. The calculations were carried out at routinely used laser wavelengths of 532 nm (ω = 0.0856 a.u.) and 1064 nm (ω = 0.0428 a.u.). The electro-optical Pockels effect (EOPE) β(-ω,ω,0) values are listed in Table S2 (Supporting Information). The results indicate that the β(-ω,ω,0) values at 532 nm are greater than the β o values. At 1064 nm, however, the β(-ω,ω,0) values are smaller than the β o values. This implies that external radiation of a certain frequency affects the NLO responses of the studied compounds.

Any putative system needs to be transparent in the UV region to behave as an efficient NLO material. Absorption spectra of pure T-GQD and the SA- and SH-doped complexes were calculated in order to investigate excitation wavelengths (λ max), oscillator strengths (fo), and transition energies. The optical properties of T-GQD complexes are modified by doping with SAs or SHs. These changes were evaluated by TD-DFT calculations at the CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level, and the results are presented in Table 7 .

Fig. 6 (a and b) show the absorption spectra of the studied complexes, indicating the peak of T-GQD at 475 nm, while higher λ max values are observed for the doped T-GQD systems. This red-shift can be ascribed to the pronounced conjugation in the SA- and SH-doped complexes [48,49]. Absorption peaks for T-GQD, Li3O@T-GQD (isomer a), Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f), BeF3@T-GQD (isomers g and i) lie in the visible region, with λ max at 475.14, 692.52, 461.05, and 553.31 nm, respectively, making them UV-transparent materials. Meanwhile, Li3O@T-GQD (isomers b and c), Li4N@T-GQD (isomers d and e), BeF3@T-GQD (isomer h), and BF4@T-GQD (isomers j, k, and l) exhibit red-shifts in the IR range, with absorption peaks at 1152.75, 999.75, 1294.06, 1412.86, 1550.33, 1304.34, 1352.66, and 1109.74 nm, respectively. This variation in absorption range can be attributed to the varying number of alkali metal and halogen atoms in the dopants. The high numbers of alkali metal and halogen atoms in Li4N@T-GQD and BF4@T-GQD, respectively, are responsible for enhanced conjugation, which increases their λ max values.

Overall, the proposed complexes are calculated to be transparent in the deep-UV region (≤200 nm). Hence, all of the reported complexes can be considered to be promising alternatives to current deep-UV-transparent NLO materials.

Reduced density gradient (RDG) is helpful for visualizing the interaction mechanism between dopants (SA, SH) and T-GQD by analyzing the type of intermolecular interactions [50,51]. Fig. 7 shows the RDG isosurfaces and NCI scattering surface images of SA- and SH-doped T-GQD complexes.

The 3D scattering RDG spectra of most stable isomers consist of blue, green, and red strips [52]. Blue strips in the region < −0.020 a.u. are associated with strong electrostatic interfaces, while green (−0.020 to 0.005 a.u.) and red bands (0.10–0.020 a.u.) are indicative of weak dispersive forces and steric repulsion [53]. It is apparent from the NCI isosurfaces of stable isomers (a, d, i, l) in Fig. 7 that a green patch is present between all dopants and T-GQD, reflecting weak van der Waals interactions, while a red combination for Li3O indicates steric repulsion between the oxygen atoms of Li3O and the nearby T-GQD atoms. Slight blue patches on the B and Be atoms in BF4 and BeF3 indicate strong electrostatic interactions with the T-GQD surface. In general, SA-doped complexes (Li3O@T-GQD and Li4N@T-GQD) containing green flakes depict van der Waals interaction between the dopant and the T-GQD surface, whilst the blue color seen for the SH-doped complexes (BF4@T-GQD and BeF3@T-GQD) indicates electrostatic interactions. Hence, NCI isosurfaces and scattering plots support the viability of interaction between the dopant and the T-GQD surface.

Various isomers of superalkali (Li3O, Li4N)- and superhalogen (BeF3, BF4)-doped tetragonal graphene quantum dot (T-GQD) complexes have been proposed. All of the isomers have been subjected to DFT (B3LYP) calculations to determine the stability, electronic properties, linear (UV/Vis), and nonlinear optical (first hyperpolarizability) responses of the optimized complexes. Geometrical parameters suggest high stability of the complexes, with interaction energies ranging from −58.30 kcal mol−1 for Li4N@T-GQD to −97.15 kcal mol−1 for BF4@T-GQD. The EH-L gap is observed to decrease by 1.63 eV for Li4N@T-GQD, imparting a favorable modification of optical properties. TD-DFT-calculated spectra reveal greater absorption in the UV/Vis and IR regions for the proposed complexes, such that they represent efficient deep-UV- and visible-range-transparent materials. Appreciable charge transfer in the complexes has been confirmed by NBO analysis, ranging from −0.95 |e| for BF4@T-GQD (isomer k) to 1.06 |e| for Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f). This can be ascribed to the establishment of new energy states, as is obvious from density of states (DOS) plots, which are also responsible for enhanced transitions giving rise to red-shifts in the UV/Vis spectra. Superalkali- and superhalogen-doping of T-GQD significantly elevates the first hyperpolarizability (β o) response, with a value of 4.5 × 104 a.u. For Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f). Moreover, the tendency of increased β o values is manifested with increasing number of superalkali and superhalogen atoms in the doped T-GQD complexes, reflecting significant charge transfer. Our results suggest that the reported superalkali- and superhalogen-doped T-GQDs may serve as excellent NLO materials, with great prospects for the superalkali-doped complex Li4N@T-GQD (isomer f) to be employed in nonlinear and optoelectronic applications.
Ali Umar: Investigation; Writing - Review & Editing.
Junaid Yaqoob: Investigation; Writing - Review & Editing.
Muhammad Usman Khan: Data Curation; Visualization; Investigation; Writing - Review & Editing; Writing - Original Draft;
Riaz Hussain: Investigation; Writing - Review & Editing.
A. Alhadhrami: Investigation; Funding acquisition; Writing - Review & Editing.
Abdulraheem S.A. Almalki: Investigation; Funding acquisition Writing - Review & Editing.
Muhammad Ramzan Saeed Ashraf Janjua: Conceptualization; Software; Writing - Original Draft; Supervision.
